1: "Go and buy a potter's clay flask (baqbûq)." The shift from Jeremiah 18's malleable unfired clay to the fired clay flask is the key Christological contrast: Jeremiah 18 is reformable clay; Jeremiah 19 is fired pottery — irreversible once broken. The divine command to buy the flask (a specific, commercially available vessel, not a lump of clay on the wheel) introduces the sign-act of irreversible judgment. Christologically, this irreversibility mirrors the eschatological judgment announced throughout the NT — the finality of judgment on those who reject the gospel's invitation (Hebrews 10:26-27; Revelation 20:11-15).
2: "Go out to the valley of Ben-hinnom, at the entrance of the Potsherd Gate." The valley of Ben-hinnom (gê ben-hinnōm) becomes Gehenna in the NT — the Greek transliteration of the Hebrew valley name. Jesus uses Gehenna more than any other NT figure as the image for final judgment (Matthew 5:22, 29-30; Jeremiah 10:28; Jeremiah 18:9; Jeremiah 23:33; Mark 9:43-48; Luke 12:5). By conducting the sign-act of irreversible judgment in this specific valley, Jeremiah establishes the symbolic geography that Christ inherits and deepens. The broken flask at the Potsherd Gate in the Hinnom Valley is the OT origin of Jesus's Gehenna-language.
3: "I am going to bring such a disaster on this place that the ears of everyone who hears about it will ring." The ṣillûl ʾoznāyw (ringing/tingling of ears) is the formula for unprecedented horror (1 Samuel 3:11; 2 Kings 21:12). Christologically, the unprecedented judgment announced for Jerusalem in 586 BCE is the type of the unprecedented judgment Jesus announces for Jerusalem in 70 CE (Luke 21:22-23: "there will be great distress on the land and wrath against this people"). The ears-ringing horror is repeated across the typological levels.
4: "Because they have abandoned me and made this a foreign place... and have filled this place with the blood of the innocent." The twofold indictment — abandoning YHWH, shedding innocent blood — is the double covenant violation that makes judgment irreversible. Christologically, the blood of the innocent that fills the valley anticipates the innocent blood shed at the cross — but with a decisive reversal. The blood of Abel cries for judgment (Genesis 4:10; Hebrews 12:24); the blood of Jesus speaks "better than the blood of Abel," not crying for judgment but securing forgiveness. The valley of innocent blood becomes, in Christ, the ground of redemption rather than condemnation.
5: "They have built the high places of Baal to burn their sons in the fire... something I never commanded, never decreed, something that never even entered my mind." The triple negation of child sacrifice — the most extreme form of idolatrous covenant violation — is YHWH's absolute disavowal of the practice. Christologically, the contrast between this abhorred child-burning and the Father's offering of his Son is total: the pagan child-sacrifice is rejected as alien to YHWH's character; the Father's offering of Christ is precisely the expression of his deepest covenant love (John 3:16). The idolatrous burning of children to Baal is the grotesque parody of what YHWH himself does in giving his Son — except that the Father gives his Son to save life, not to extinguish it.
6: "Therefore the days are coming when this place will no longer be called Tophet or the valley of Ben-hinnom, but the valley of Slaughter." The renaming of Tophet as the "valley of Slaughter" is a prophetic omen-name: the place of false sacrifice becomes the place of true judgment. Christologically, the NT's use of Gehenna (from Ben-hinnom) as the image of final judgment completes this renaming: the valley where Israel played at sacrifice (child-burning) becomes the symbolic site of genuine divine reckoning. Jesus's Gehenna-warnings are the fullest development of this Jeremianic renaming.
7: "I will empty out the plans of Judah and Jerusalem in this place." The verb bāqaq (to empty) is the same root as baqbûq (the flask of v1) — YHWH will empty out Judah's plans just as the flask will be emptied and then smashed. The wordplay (baqbûq/bāqaq) is the sign-act's own linguistic interpretation. Christologically, the emptying of human plans before divine judgment mirrors the emptying of Christ at the incarnation (Philippians 2:7: ekenōsen, "he emptied himself") — except that Christ's self-emptying is voluntary and redemptive, while Judah's emptying is involuntary and penal.
8: "I will make this city a scene of horror and contempt; all who pass by it will be horrified and will hiss in scorn." The formula of horror and hissing recurs from Jeremiah 18:16. Christologically, the passersby who mock the ruined city (Lamentations 1:12: "Is it nothing to you, all you who pass by?") are a type of the passersby who mock the crucified Christ (Matthew 27:39: "those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads"). The lament formula of the destroyed city becomes the lament formula of the suffering servant.
9: "I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and daughters." The siege-cannibalism announced as the extreme of covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:53-57; Lamentations 4:10) is the most shocking element of the oracle. Christologically, this extreme physical curse is the negative context that makes Jesus's eucharistic language scandalous and profound in John 6:53-56: "Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you." Where covenant violation produces the horror of literal cannibalism in the siege, the new covenant produces the mystery of eucharistic communion — feeding on the life of Christ rather than destroying one another's lives.
10: "Then break the flask in front of the men who have come with you." The performative destruction of the flask before witnesses is a legal and prophetic act — a sign-act that enacts the word it illustrates. Christologically, the public nature of the sign-act ("in front of the men") anticipates the public nature of the cross: Christ was crucified openly, "publicly portrayed as crucified" (Galatians 3:1), not in private. The sign-act performed before witnesses enacts the prophetic word; the cross enacted before witnesses fulfills the prophetic word.
11: "I will smash this people and this city just as one smashes a potter's vessel, so that it can never be repaired again." The irreparability (lōʾ yûḵal lĕhērāpēʾ ʿôd, it cannot be repaired again) is the key contrast with Jeremiah 18: the unfired clay can be remade; the fired and broken flask cannot. Christologically, this irreparability is the language of eschatological judgment (Revelation 2:27: "as when earthen pots are broken in pieces"; Revelation 19:15: Jesus smashes the nations with a rod of iron). The broken flask that cannot be repaired is the OT type of the final judgment that cannot be reversed.
12: "This is what I will do to this place and its inhabitants... I will make this whole city like Tophet." The scope extends from the specific site (Tophet) to the whole city — the sign-act becomes a comprehensive oracle. Christologically, the expansion from a specific place to the whole city mirrors the NT's expansion of judgment and salvation from specific to universal: Christ's death at a specific place (Golgotha, outside Jerusalem) becomes the definitive event for the whole world (John 12:32: "when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself").
13: "The houses of Jerusalem and the houses of the kings of Judah will be defiled just like Tophet — all the houses on whose rooftops they burned incense to all the starry host of heaven." The rooftop incense to the host of heaven (astral worship) is the Babylonian-influenced cult that penetrated even the royal houses. Christologically, this worship of created cosmic powers (stars and astral deities) is precisely what Paul addresses as the enslaved state from which Christ liberates (Galatians 4:3, 9: "weak and worthless elementary principles... enslaved to those that by nature are not gods"; Colossians 2:8, 20). The cosmic powers worshiped on Jerusalem's rooftops are the OT form of the "elemental spirits" that Christ disarmed at the cross (Colossians 2:15).
14: "Jeremiah came back from Tophet, where the LORD had sent him to prophesy, and stood in the court of the LORD's temple." The prophetic return from the place of enacted judgment to the temple court — to speak the same word in the most sacred space — mirrors the pattern of prophetic courage that Christ embodies in his temple-cleansing (Matthew 21:12-13; John 2:13-17). The prophet who proclaims judgment in the valley then moves to the temple; Jesus who drives out the money-changers in the temple uses the same prophetic-judgment vocabulary: "My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of thieves" — citing Jeremiah 7:11.
15: "I am going to bring upon this city and all its surrounding towns every disaster I have announced against them, because they have stubbornly refused to listen to my words." The summary statement of irreversible judgment due to stubborn refusal is the final word of the Tophet oracle. Christologically, Jesus uses the same "stubbornly refused" language in his final lament over Jerusalem: "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... How often would I have gathered your children together... and you were not willing!" (Matthew 23:37). The accumulation of refused prophetic words reaches its climax in the refusal of the Word himself.
